--- SOURCE 1: [PDF] Straight Talk About Veterinary Telemedicine and the Veterinary ... ---
URL: https://leg.colorado.gov/sites/default/files/images/committees/dr._lori_teller_-_veterinary_telemedicine_.pdf

SUMMARY:
(Human) Medical Malpractice Risks. • Over 4-year period, one liability company found 66% telemedicine-related claims related to misdiagnosis.

FULL CONTENT:
Straight Talk About Veterinary Telemedicine and the Veterinary Workforce Lori Teller, DVM, DABVP (canine/feline), CVJ Texas A&M University School of Veterinary Medicine Clinical Professor, Telehealth AVMA Immediate Past President September 11, 2023 Telehealth reduces barriers to care without opening the VCPR Telehealth is the overarching term that encompasses all uses of technology to remotely gather and deliver health information, advice, education, and care. This also encompasses teletriage.
• Teleadvice provides owners with basic answers to their questions and education around preventive care. No VCPR required.
• Teletriage enables determination of urgency (i.e., whether the animal needs to be immediately seen by a veterinarian or not). No VCPR required.
• Telemedicine can be used—once the VCPR is established through an in-person examination or premise visit—to facilitate progress checks and owner compliance. Saves time and money.
• Emergency clauses present in state practice acts allow treatment in the case where immediate intervention is required and/or address unique needs (e.g., geography), while maintaining the integrity of the VCPR.
Definitions: Telemedicine • Have been doing for a long, long, time.
• Important to do it with a veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR) established in-person.
• In-person visit provides critical information about both the patient and the client.
• Knowledge about both allows veterinarians to deliver an effective treatment plan the owner can follow.
• Animals with gaps in seeing a veterinarian are more likely to have acute and complex problems that neither an electronic VCPR, nor a prescription for flea control are going to solve.
• “Some care is better than no care” isn’t true when you have the wrong diagnosis and wrong treatment plan.
• An accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment plan from the start reduces the number of visits needed and reduces costs to the owner.
Because veterinary medicine is frequently compared to pediatrics… • AAP/ATA Guidelines: Telehealth services should not be provided to children under two years of age in their home or other non-clinical setting except when the provider or their surrogate has a previously established in-person relationship with the patient or when the PCMH has referred them for subspecialty consultation. • Curfman, et al, 2022: Care within the medical home: Support for the use of telehealth within the medical home recognizes that the medical home offers continuity and the prudent use of health care resources, avoiding fragmented and episodic care delivered without such coordination. It is important to make a distinction between telemedicine provided in the PCMH vs a stand-alone DTC platform that targets a specific problem.
It is also important to distinguish between DTC sites that provide teleadvice and teletriage services vs those that prioritize product sales over appropriate care. Patient-Centered Medical Home vs Direct-to-Consumer Telemedicine DTC Veterinary Care: Telehealth-oriented TeleHEALTH companies provide general advice and/or triage services *Screenshots (2 companies--identifiers removed) acquired April 2023 DTC Veterinary Care: Product-oriented *Text from screenshots acquired April 2023 from various DTC companies--modified to remove identifying info.
“At that moment the technological imperative transforms the healing profession into a healthcare enterprise and our patients become a means to an end.” ~Fleming, Edison, Pak (Human) Medical Malpractice Risks • Over 4-year period, one liability company found 66% telemedicine-related claims related to misdiagnosis • Harvard Medical School found 68% telemedicine-related claims due to failed diagnosis • Misdiagnosis accounts for 47% claims associated with in-person visits • Consequences of misdiagnosis were high • 44% loss of life (most common consequence) • 60% malpractice claims resulted in settlement or award to plaintiff • Mitigating risks is challenging because unknown how many cases settled before being heard in court ~Rowland 2022 Challenges in Human and Veterinary Virtual Care • Medicare data indicate that >25% beneficiaries lack digital access at home • Highest proportion amongst those of lowest economic status and those in communities of color • Disparities in digital access continue to exist • Clients/Patients who have financial barriers to care may also have transportation barriers and limited internet access • Home visits are most essential for this population • Inappropriate prescribing of antimicrobials is more prevalent during telemedicine visits • As DTC telemedicine companies continue to expand, it will be imperative for health care professionals and policy makers to monitor these treatment options to ensure convenience does not come at the expense of quality. ~Jain What does a virtual VCPR deliver?
• Missed or delayed diagnoses o Patient suffering o Public health risks o High-consequence disease economic impacts that can range into the billions of dollars • Confusion for veterinarians and clients due to differing state and federal requirements for prescribing • Increased liability • As learned from human medicine o Reductions in preventive care o Pill mills and fraud More about the workforce: proposed solutions & likely effects • Already happening!
• Between 2022 and 2030 the number of companion animal veterinarians will grow by more than 20%—from about 80,000 to more than 98,000.
• In addition to this 20% increase… o Veterinary schools are adding seats to their existing programs.
o 10 new veterinary schools are in various stages of development.
• Rural areas and certain segments of the profession—food animal, equine, shelters, academia, and some veterinary specialties— continue to be challenged.
• Hiring more staff substantively impacts practices financially.
• Problematic when market demand downshifts.
• Already seeing indications of a downshift: reductions in visits, new patients, and new client numbers, YOY Increasing the number of veterinarians Proposed solutions & likely effects • Functions overlap services already competently delivered by veterinarians, veterinary technicians, and veterinary technician specialists.
• Accredited educational programs, national testing and licensing, and needed state and federal practice authorities, are likely to take decades.
• First NP program was established nearly 60 years ago, and human medicine still struggles with issues created by moving forward without standardized education, credentialling, and licensure. This includes educational programs that do not prepare students appropriately and put patients at risk. • Instead, fully engage credentialed veterinary technicians and veterinary technician specialists as part of a veterinarian-led team and pay appropriately.
Veterinary Midlevel Position Proposed solutions & likely effects Midlevel practitioner (MLP) • In human medicine, recent large-scale studies1,2,3 have found: o MLPs significantly increase resource utilization, but achieve worse patient outcomes o Net medical costs are higher o MLPs have significantly more diagnosis-related malpractice allegations than physicians 1Chan DC, Chen Y. The productivity of professions: evidence from the emergency department. Working Paper 30608, National Bureau of Economic Research, Issued October 2022, revised June 2023.
2Batson BN, Crosby SN, Fitzpatrick JM. Targeting value-based care with physician-led care teams. JMSMA 2022;63(1):19-21.
3Brock DM, Nicholson JG, Hooker RS. Physician assistant and nurse practitioner malpractice trends. Med Care Res and Rev 2017;74(5):613-624.
4American Medical Association, 2021.
Proposed solutions and likely effects Areas and practice segment with special needs: • Clear workforce shortages in Rural areas Food animal Equine Academia Shelters Some specialties Public health • Each faces unique challenges and needs targeted solutions.
• Neither simply increasing the number of veterinarians, nor creating a midlevel practitioner is going to fix these issues.
Proposed solutions and likely effects Improving practice efficiency: • Subject of recent article in JAVMA • Study findings suggest efforts to optimize efficiency could allow companion animal practices to meet service demands without needing to hire more staff.
• Highly efficient practices use the least resources (inputs) to achieve maximal production (outputs).
• Improvements leading to better efficiency include o Engaging support staff to full potential o Fully utilizing technology (e.g., PIMS, digital inventory management, online scheduling, telehealth) o Better leadership and management Questions?

--- SOURCE 3: [PDF] Veterinary Telemedicine 101 & The Ethics of Telemedicine ---
URL: https://scav.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/SCAV-Telehealth-101.pdf

SUMMARY:
telemedicine is misdiagnosis; 44% of those complaints involved death of the patient. • Particular risk associated with direct-to-consumer.

FULL CONTENT:
Veterinary Telemedicine 101 & The Ethics of Telemedicine South Carolina Association of Veterinarians March 24, 2024 Lori Teller, DVM, DABVP (canine/feline), CVJ Clinical Professor, Telehealth Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences lteller@tamu.edu Telehealth (aka Connected Care) • Encompassing term • Use of technology to remotely gather and deliver health information, advice, education, and patient care • All telemedicine is telehealth, but not all telehealth is telemedicine • Two categories for communication with animal owners • Without a veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR) • General, non-patient-specific advice • Teletriage • With a VCPR • Diagnosis • Patient-specific treatment recommendations • Prescriptions Why Embrace Connected Care?
• Additional way to serve clients • Provides convenient way to meet client needs • Continuity in patient care, including improved client compliance • Can help remove barriers to care • Better team utilization, especially veterinary technicians • Increase practice efficiency • Additional source of income • Self care • Supports setting boundaries • Opportunities for work/life integration • Supports, but does not replace, in-person visits and relationships What types of cases?
• Triage • Surgical rechecks • Chronic conditions (e.g., atopy, diabetes) • Wound care • Behavior • Movement disorders, arthritis • Uncomplicated vomiting and diarrhea (e.g., dietary indiscretions, BAR patients) • Hospice care • Environmental monitoring • Special circumstances (e.g., transportation issues, language barriers) *Use in cases that you are comfortable handling virtually **Apply standard of care consistent with in-person visit Language of Connected Care VCPR NOT Required Teleadvice • Any health information, opinion, or guidance that is not specific to a particular animal’s health, illness, or injury • Does not provide a diagnosis, prognosis, or treatment recommendation(s) for a specific patients or group of patients Teletriage • Safe, appropriate and timely assessment and management (immediate referral to a veterinarian or not) of animal patients via electronic consultation with their owner • Under conditions of uncertainty and urgency • A diagnosis is not rendered, treatment is not provided VCPR NOT Required Teleconsulting • Primary care/attending veterinarian uses tools of telehealth to communicate with a veterinary specialist or another qualified expert to gain insights and advice on the care of a patient • No VCPR involved; between professionals VCPR NOT Required Tele-education • Application of information and communication technologies to distance learning • Can be synchronous or asynchronous VCPR IS Required Telemedicine • Use tools of telehealth to exchange information about a particular patient’s or group of patients’ clinical health status electronically from one site to another • Patient-specific • Involves diagnosis, treatment recommendations, and prognosis VCPR IS Required ePrescribing • Electronic generation, transmission, and filling of a medical prescription or order • State and federal requirements, including those for prescribing controlled substances, ELDU, and VFDs • Requires a VCPR VCPR IS Required Tele-supervision • Supervision of non-DVM staff, using media such as audio or audio-video conference, text messaging, and email • Potential additional value in rural and remote areas • Regulatory guidance is generally lacking (direct or indirect supervision), so caution is prudent VCPR may be required Remote Monitoring • Use remote devices and software (e.g., wearables, environmental sensors, applications) to collect and transmit data from a patient in one location to the veterinarian in another location for assessment and recommendations • Some devices are designed for clinical use (within VCPR), others are marketed directly to the consumer (outside of a VCPR) • Data transmission may be synchronous or asynchronous Laws, Rules, Regulations and Other Stuff Know the rules • State veterinary practice act, rules, policy • South Carolina uses the same definition of the VCPR as the FDA • State pharmacy laws and regs • Federal prescribing laws and regs • Federal VCPR requirements (FDA, USDA, DEA, HISA) • Federal/state/local medical record and retention requirements • State veterinary client/patient confidentiality laws • State and federal controlled substance laws • Consent • State telehealth laws, regs, policies Basic components of the VCPR • Veterinarian has assumed responsibility for making medical judgments regarding the health of an animal (or group of animals) and the need for medical treatment • The client has agreed to follow the instructions of the veterinarian • There is sufficient knowledge of the animal by the veterinarian to initiate at least a general or preliminary diagnosis of the medical condition of the animal • Veterinarian is readily available for follow up in case of adverse reactions or failure of the regimen or therapy Federal law requires in-person visit to create VCPR • FDA • Extralabel drug use (includes compounded products and FDA-approved OTC products) • Estimated that 60-80% drugs used in vet med are ELDU • Veterinary Feed Directives • USDA • Use of certain types of biologics • Issuing Certificates of Veterinary Inspection (CVIs) • Evaluating and testing for certain diseases • FTC • Horses covered under the Horseracing Integrity and Safety Act (HISA) Given that the Federal veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR) definition (21 CFR 530.3(i)) requires animal examination and/or medically appropriate and timely visits to the premises where the animal(s) are kept, the Federal VCPR definition cannot be met solely through telemedicine.
― FDA, 2017, 2022 Federal VCPR: establishing vs maintaining • A VCPR meeting the federal definition is required for ELDU (FDA), Veterinary Feed Directives (FDA), and use of certain types of biologics (USDA) “Such a relationship can exist only when the veterinarian has recently seen and is personally acquainted with the keeping and care of the animal by virtue of an examination of the animal, and/or by medically appropriate and timely visits to the premises where the animals are kept.” • FDA has interpreted its own language to mean that a VCPR cannot be established by telemedicine.
• FDA has also said that telemedicine may be used to maintain the VCPR State VCPR • The basic components are included within the federal VCPR • When states began creating their own definitions, most adopted language similar to the federal VCPR • 43 states use language that is essentially the same as FDA • 6 states (AZ, CA, ID [cannot prescribe without in-person examination],NJ, VT, VA) allow a VCPR to be established virtually • 2 jurisdictions have no VCPR provisions (MI [requires in-person examination prior to providing telehealth services], NY) Telehealth and the VCPR (in general) • You DON’T need to establish a VCPR to provide: • General education • General advice • Teletriage • Teleconsulting • You DO need to establish a VCPR to: • Provide a diagnosis • Make treatment recommendations • Provide a prognosis • Prescribe medications • Alter a current treatment protocol Eliminating initial in-person visit to establish VCPR = unacceptable risks impacts on health and welfare of animals, public health, food safety • Misdiagnoses, delayed diagnoses, missed diagnoses • Zoonotic diseases (e.g., rabies, leptospirosis) • High-consequence diseases (e.g., HPAI, FMD) • Failure to identify co-existing conditions • Provision of unnecessary or inappropriate drugs and other medical products • Antimicrobials, opioids • Can drive restrictions on access to drugs, shortages • Emergence of “pill mills” • Conflicts in treatment due to disconnected providers • More complaints and liability ― PLIT receiving veterinary licensing board complaints and malpractice claims • Significant enforcement challenges for state veterinary medical boards (enforceable regulations, investigations, remedies) Why a strong and consistent VCPR matters: Patient & Client Protection • An in-person examination or premise visit provides critical information about both the patient and the client • Relationships are the foundations of trust and practice • Clients often misinterpret clinical signs • Most common malpractice complaint in human telemedicine is misdiagnosis; 44% of those complaints involved death of the patient • Particular risk associated with direct-to-consumer (product driven, pill mill) telemedicine • Are you a veterinarian…or a vending machine?
• Differences in federal and state requirements create confusion for veterinarians and clients Why a strong and consistent VCPR matters: Veterinary Access to Medications • Efforts at the state, federal, and international levels to ensure good antimicrobial stewardship • LOTS of pressure on veterinary medicine, because people do not always understand how we use antimicrobials • Perception of overuse in animal agriculture • Shifting attention to companion animal medicine • Multiple studies suggest overprescribing when patient-provider relationships are established electronically • In-person VCPR has protected access to medically important antimicrobials at the state, federal, and global levels because regulators recognize this as contributing to good oversight • Similar scenario for controlled substances Impact of telemedicine on traditional VCPR Survey of pet owners using a telehealth-only website (no VCPR required) • 91% of owners who have a veterinarian would use telemedicine if offered by their veterinarian • 60% of telehealth consultations resulted in recommendation for in-person visit • 68% of those clients did follow-up with their veterinarian • 89% of those felt better informed and 87% felt better able to communicate during follow-up • Primary veterinarian agreed with recommendation of telehealth service in 82% of cases. • Telemedicine can enhance the traditional veterinary visit. Roca 2019 Owner satisfaction with video rechecks after surgical sterilization • Owners equally satisfied with rechecks performed in-person vs videoconference • Owners reported that dogs seen virtually were less anxious than when in-person • Surgical complication rates were similar between groups • No dog seen virtually needed to then be seen in-person • People who utilized virtual visit would prefer this for similar appointments in future Bishop 2018 Small animal practitioners' perceptions of synchronous telemedicine during COVID-19 • Practitioner utilization of telemedicine pre-pandemic: 4% • During pandemic: 30% • Most common applications: • Ongoing case management • Dermatology • Behavior • Post-surgical care • Virtual visits took less time and generated less revenue (some practitioners did not charge) • Practitioners felt that it was more difficult to foster relationships and convey information Bishop 2021 Veterinarians’ perceptions of pandemic-related influences on veterinary telehealth • # of companion animal practices offering telehealth services tripled (12% pre-pandemic to 38% during summer 2020) • Most common issues handled via telehealth: derm, triage, GI • Most common challenges: technology issues, client interest • Most perceived benefits: safety, positive feedback from clients, efficiency • Most common concern of veterinarian: delayed treatment • Pet owners' preferences: in-person visit> drop-off> phone call> video chat Dublin 2021 Telemedicine for food animal safety and production: • Improved technology: • Whisper stethoscope • REDI (remote early disease identification) system • ECG pill to measure heart rate • Ear tags measure body temperature • Integrated monitoring: • Improve individual animal health • Prevent/reduce widespread disease outbreak • Natural or terrorism • Improve disease reporting • Alleviate strain of rural veterinary shortage • Mentorship for rural and solo practitioners • Digital imaging/pathology/necropsy Teleconsultation: help when you need it • Referral is not always an option—because of geography, finances, weather conditions, etc.
• Consulting with remote specialist to help triage patient • Early referral may improve outcome • Referral may not be necessary • Recent grads, or more experienced DVMs with “zebra” cases, benefit from extra support • Primary care doctors can get needed reassurance, learn new skills, and improve their knowledge base • Tele-consults can increase bond between client and regular veterinarian • If referral needed, the client will already have some knowledge of the specialist’s ability to help Teleconsulting • Cytopathology • Remote necropsy • Imaging • Internal medicine • Dermatology • Surgical management • Injuries • Critical care • Behavior • Cardiology Remote Monitoring and Wearable Devices • Collect and analyze data synchronously or asynchronously • Data can be retrieved from remote locations on multiple devices, including smartphones, tablets, or computers • Tracking trends can help identify warning signs and lead to earlier interventions • Tracking data may improve client compliance • Information obtained can help tailor appropriate preventive care to pet’s needs • Information obtained can be used to manage chronic conditions, such as diabetes and arthritis Courtesy of AAHA & AVMA Examples: VetMeasure harness to monitor vitals, microchips to measure body temperature, Freestyle Libre continuous glucose monitors Remote monitoring Chronic Care & Disease Management • Clients often collect their pet’s data, using simple-to-use technological devices • Blood glucose • Activity monitors • Digital therapeutics • Primarily used for monitoring chronic diseases or conditions • Digestive • Cardiovascular/respiratory • Dermatologic • Behavioral • Musculoskeletal • Infectious diseases • Neoplasia • Palliative care/hospice Pet Insurance, Health Care Services, and Telehealth • Embrace • Nationwide • Trupanion • ASPCA • FirstVet • Banfield Optimum Wellness Plan with Vet Chat • PetFirst • VetsChoice • Pets Best • PetIQ • Lemonade • Petco • Chewy, Walmart… Research validates the appropriate use of virtual care in veterinary medicine Ethical Considerations Veterinary ethics, as well as lessons learned from human medicine Ethical Considerations • Standard of care • Convenience • Quality • Consent • Data security and privacy • Misdiagnosis and delay of appropriate care • Equity and Accessibility • Prescribing of medications • Continuity of care • Reliability of technology Opportunities & Challenges Opportunities: • Reduce barriers to care • Providing care during disasters or large disease outbreaks • Triage • General advice/education • Tech appointments • Continuity of care • Chronic disease monitoring • Enhance bond between veterinarian and client Challenges: • Lack of or limited physical exam • Insufficient medical information • Maintaining standard of care • Inappropriate and/or overprescribing • Cybersecurity • Client/patient confidentiality • Data, including financial, security • Technology limitations • Licensure, regulatory, legal considerations • Reimbursement One reason to consider the ethics of veterinary telemedicine Convenience and Quality • Americans place a high value on choice, availability, comfort, and autonomy • Important to ask whether quality is being compromised in the tradeoff for convenience • Ex. Coughing dog with early-stage valvular disease • Ex. Abdominal pain vs back pain Just Distribution of Care • Potential to increased access for populations who struggle with mobility or transportation issues • Patients may not need to take time off from work or find childcare • Expanding telehealth can result in 2-tiered system for clients already disadvantaged • Exacerbating structural inequities (made more evident during COVID) • Specifically, the very clients who struggle to get to clinic are the ones whose pets most stand to benefit from in-person visit • Clients with least comfort (elderly; lower tech literacy) unduly guided to telemedicine under guise of improved access • Introducing telemedicine without other structural supports (broadband internet, appropriate device, tech support) amplifies disparities Direct-to-Consumer Telemedicine Let’s take a deeper dive into what it is and the ethics of it It is important to make a distinction between telemedicine provided in the PCMH vs a stand-alone DTC platform that targets a specific problem.
It is also important to distinguish between DTC sites that provide teleadvice and teletriage services vs those that prioritize product sales over appropriate care. Patient-Centered Medical Home vs Direct-to-Consumer Telemedicine Patient-Centered Medical Home • Provides primary and preventive health services • Creates and maintains the provider-patient relationship ― Each patient has a personal physician ― Care is physician-directed with joint decision making with patient • Coordinates and integrates care with specialists or other health care professionals as needed by the patient ― Addresses various life stages and acute or chronic disease • Information technology is utilized to ensure exchange of all health care information, so the patient receives indicated care when and where needed by the appropriate provider.
Potential Conflicts of Interest with Human DTC Telemedicine Ethically beneficial • Convenience • Young • Affluent urban areas • Access care in off-hours • Geography and transportation barriers are decreased • +/- less expensive in some cases Ethically problematic • Physician hired by companies that exist to sell product • Online DTC companies only make money if MD/DO writes Rx • Potential decrease in public trust of doctors (don’t have patient’s best interest in mind) • Does not appear to mitigate major barriers, such as language, digital literacy, or insurance acceptance DTC Telemedicine • DTC telehealth companies pushed to prescribe outside conventional Doctor-Patient Relationship • With ”internet doctoring” patients may not inform PCP about telehealth visits or new Rx • Minimal screening for duplicate therapies • Increased risk of drug/drug or drug/disease interactions • DTC telemedicine visits focused on limited set of conditions with goal of selling product • Patient may never have live/synchronous consult with health care provider • Patient completes questionnaire that is reviewed by provider for contraindications and, if none, medication is mailed • Sometimes no provider is involved, and it’s all done via a chatbot • Companies are sales-oriented, not care-oriented • Profit-centered, not patient-centered • Not necessarily offering the most appropriate treatment for the issue Ethics of DTC Telederm • Problems exist with confirming client/patient ID, ascertaining medical hx, provision of inadequate counseling on adverse effects, confirming appropriate licensure of provider • Patients often not able to choose provider/prescriber • Lack of communication with PCPs or integration with health care systems • Conflict of interest: companies profit from patient fees and again from medications/products sold • Evidence suggests most clients not from medically underserved areas • AAD position: “have an existing physician-provider relationship” or “be a part of an integrated health care delivery system where patient already receives care” • Every effort for Rx only to be rendered after adequate hx and exam taken, as well as having prior doctor-patient relationship, without prioritizing profit Korman 2021 (Ethics Journal Club in JAAD) Telederm consults as a triage tool to improve access • 30K patients; 55K lesions in 1 year • 53% patients could be triaged using TD to provide treatment suggestions to PCP for management of case • More serious cases referred to Derm; shortened wait times overall • Potential to miss important lesions • PCP expected to follow-up or refer if new lesions develop or condition worsens ~Giavina-Bianchi 2020 Veterinary Derm: Barriers to Allergy-Specific Immunotherapy (ASIT) for canine atopic dermatitis • Response to pharm management, cost, PC veterinarians’ perception of inability to reduce pharm dependency • >30% PC DVMs rarely/never refer to derm because of cost, availability of serological testing, perception of limited benefits • Telederm as cooperative effort with dog’s primary DVM and client would improve referrals and uptake of ASIT • Educational opportunities to reach referral base and increase awareness of benefits of ASIT ~Flanagan 2020 Challenges in Human and Veterinary Virtual Care • Medicare data indicate that >25% beneficiaries lack digital access at home • Highest proportion amongst those of lowest economic status and those in communities of color • Disparities in digital access continue to exist • Clients/Patients who have financial barriers to care may also have transportation barriers and limited internet access • Home visits are most essential for this population • Inappropriate prescribing of antimicrobials is more prevalent during telemedicine visits • As DTC telemedicine companies continue to expand, it will be imperative for health care professionals and policy makers to monitor these treatment options to ensure convenience does not come at the expense of quality. ~Jain Misleading to compare human and veterinary medicine • Human and veterinary patients are different • Animals hide signs of illness and injury • Even well-intentioned animal owners incorrectly recognize or fail to report health issues • Over-reliance on owner reports can lead to diagnostic missteps and inappropriate treatment • American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) does not support the use of telemedicine for children younger than two years old without a previous in-person visit • Drugs are regulated differently “Telehealth services should not be provided to children under two years of age in their home or other non-clinical setting except when the provider or their surrogate has a previously established in-person relationship with the patient or when the Patient-Centered Medical Home has referred them for subspecialty consultation” “Support for the use of telehealth within the medical home recognizes that the medical home offers continuity and the prudent use of healthcare resources, avoiding fragmented and episodic care delivered without such coordination” ― AAP, Section on Telehealth Care, Committee on Practice and Ambulatory Medicine, Committee on Pediatric Workforce; 2017, 2021 Telehealth Turned into Marketing Gold Mine • For pharma companies, online prescribing has now become a powerful tool to drive sales, sending hundreds of thousands of patients to the (online) clinic at the moment they're most primed to ask for a specific prescription. • The company that runs the Linzess platform, Populus Media, says that across its telehealth prescribing programs, more than 90% of eligible patients who finish intake forms get a script for the drug they clicked on. (Linzess is medication for IBS in humans) • "We're going to make sure that we take somebody while our brand is in their headspace and drive them to have a conversation with a physician," said Populus co-founder Jeffrey Erb. • "When you enter our funnel, you're going to end up with a prescription if you want the medication," said Prescribery's chief operating and financial officer Ross Pope, with the exception of 10% to 15% of patients who might get a different recommendation if they're disqualified by medical contraindications or drug interactions. • For patients, this sounds like convenience, autonomy, and a way to feel empowered in their health care.
• Is appropriate care being sacrificed? Who is protecting the public?
“This is Pharma’s Dream” • Health policy experts warn that this prioritizes profits at the cost of poor care and potentially unnecessary and costly prescriptions • "This is a way of making prescription drugs about as available as over-the- counter drugs," said Georgetown University Medical Center professor Adriane Fugh-Berman, who directs the center's project on evidence-based prescribing and medical marketing. "This is pharma's dream." • They worry marketing will manipulate patients into self-diagnosing disorders or seeking out specific drugs, tempted by the perception of control and speed. And when a virtual visit is focused on eligibility for a single drug, they say patients are unlikely to receive a full consideration of the alternatives. • Notably, pharmaceutical companies disclaim liability for physicians' recommendations FDA & DOJ Enforcement Actions—Can we trust doctors?
• HealthRight telemedicine scheme (2018) • Call with employee interfered with creation of doctor-patient relationship • Employee recorded patient answers on questionnaire, along with suggested medication, and forwarded to doctor for approval • Criminal indictment • AidAccess (2019) • Dispensing unapproved and misbranded products via the internet • Warning letter • DOJ action (Sept 2021) • Telemedicine company executives allegedly paid doctors and nurses to order unnecessary medical equipment, diagnostics, and pain medicines without any interaction with patient • Labor lawsuit (Apr 2022) • Alleges Cerebral planned to increase customer retention by prescribing stimulants to 100% ADHD patients • Providers felt pressure to prescribe stimulants after initial 30-minute visit • FDA has broad regulatory and enforcement authority over drugs and medical devices • In addition to State Boards, FDA has oversight of telemedicine-related activities FDA advises consumers “to avoid online pharmacies that allow them to buy drugs without a prescription or by completing an online questionnaire.” DTC Veterinary Care: Telehealth-oriented TeleHEALTH companies provide general advice and/or triage services *Screenshots from different companies (identifiers removed) DTC Veterinary Care: Product-oriented *Text from various DTC companies--modified to remove identifying info.
“At that moment the technological imperative transforms the healing profession into a healthcare enterprise and our patients become a means to an end.” ~Fleming, Edison, Pak DTC Veterinary Care: Product-oriented (Human) Medical Malpractice Risks • Over 4-year period, one liability company found 66% telemedicine-related claims related to misdiagnosis • Another insurance company identified 71% telemedicine claims related to misdiagnosis • Harvard Medical School found 68% telemedicine-related claims due to failed diagnosis • Misdiagnosis accounts for 47% claims associated with in-person visits • Consequences of misdiagnosis with telemedicine were high • 44% loss of life (most common consequence) • 60% malpractice claims resulted in settlement or award to plaintiff • AVMA PLIT is now receiving board complaints and malpractice claims for telemedicine • Mitigating risks is challenging because unknown how many cases settled before being heard in court Lessons from the Pandemic • Telehealth can decrease barriers to care • Provide teleadvice • Triage patients who may need emergent/urgent care • Connect owners/animals to in-person care • Rural clients interested in telemedicine for chronic disease management • Decrease stress on patient • Want support from veterinary team when utilizing telemedicine • Continued concerns by veterinarians and client regarding misdiagnosis and delayed treatment © Copyright 2022. American Veterinary Medical Association. All rights reserved. Mission: To collaborate across the veterinary and animal health industry to enhance and expand care by leveraging technology, while safeguarding the welfare of animals and people. • Launched by AVMA, Veterinary Study Groups, and Merck Animal Health • A broadly supported veterinary and animal health industry effort • State, allied, and other veterinary organizations • NAVTA • national practices • independent practices • animal health companies (pharma, distributors) • telehealth service providers • Almost 70 organizations strong and growing • www.avma.org/ConnectedCare Resources • AVMA Telehealth Resource Center: https://www.avma.org/resources-tools/animal-health-and-welfare/telehealth-telemedicine-veterinary-practice • AVMA guidelines for the use of telehealth in veterinary practice: https://www.avma.org/sites/default/files/2021-01/AVMA-Veterinary-Telehealth-Guidelines.pdf • AAHA/AVMA telehealth guidelines for small animal practice: https://www.avma.org/sites/default/files/2021-01/AVMA-Veterinary-Telehealth-Guidelines.pdf Questions?

--- SOURCE 4: [PDF] May 15, 2025 - HB 4220 - Michigan Veterinary Medical Association ---
URL: https://www.house.mi.gov/Document/?DocumentId=52883&DocumentType=CommitteeTestimony

SUMMARY:
We also have concerns with the accuracy of a telehealth-only diagnosis. The quality of a telehealth visit depends on the internet connection

--- SOURCE 5: Telehealth for pets? It's the cat's meow, a Texas lawmaker says. ---

FULL CONTENT:
## Key coverage

# Telehealth for pets? It’s the cat’s meow, a Texas lawmaker says.

Animal health care experts raised concern that telehealth would lead to misdiagnosis and erode what little care already exists in rural Texas.

![Genna Deleon, 32, vet technician, holds Roco, an 11-year-old Silky Terrier, during a wellness visit at the Spay Neuter Network Crandall clinic in Crandall on Sept. 27, 2024.](https://thumbnails.texastribune.org/ZFWUNrZs6EpFQDb-jwke19mJGyw=/850x570/smart/filters:quality(75)/https://static.texastribune.org/media/files/754c7a2162c273a051af1c22c5f7a798/0927%20Animal%20Welfare%20DR%20TT%2020.jpg)

*[Sign up for The Brief](https://www.texastribune.org/newsletters/the-brief/?utm_medium=website&utm_source=trib-ads-owned&utm_campaign=trib-marketing&utm_term=inline-CTA-brief), The Texas Tribune’s daily newsletter that keeps readers up to speed on the most essential Texas news.*

LUBBOCK — Telehealth is going to the dogs.

Or, at least, [state Sen. Nathan Johnson](https://www.texastribune.org/directory/nathan-johnson/) wants it to. The Dallas Democrat has proposed legislation that would expand telehealth practices to veterinary medicine.

Animal health experts, however, are not throwing him a bone, and say the bill could do more harm than good.

At a Senate committee hearing Monday, Johnson said his [legislation](https://capitol.texas.gov/BillLookup/History.aspx?LegSess=89R&Bill=SB1442) would update Texas law to align with a court decision that allows veterinarians to establish a veterinary-client-patient relationship through electronic means. Texas law effectively bans using veterinary telemedicine without an initial physical examination.

Dr. Nancy Carter, president of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association, said the bill is framed as a “magic bullet” to fix problems — from rising costs for animal owners and limited access to veterinary care. She called it a false promise. There are conditions that could be misdiagnosed without physically examining an animal and listening to its heart and lungs, she said.

“This runs the risk of missing a serious illness or prescribing medications that do not treat the animal's condition or even threaten its health,” Carter said.

Colleen Vera, an animal rescue volunteer and vice chair of the Tyler County Republican Party, supported the bill. She said when she moved to rural East Texas, she found a great vet. However, the vet provides only basic care. This often leaves Vera’s rescue animals and pets without the medication they need.

“I realize most pet owners will never use telemedicine, and it won’t reduce the need to see a vet in person for vaccines and testing,” Vera said. “But for those of us in rescue, especially in rural Texas, telemedicine would be such a blessing.”

Dr. Monica Janacek, a rancher and veterinarian in La Grange, testified against the bill. She worries about dangerous diseases that could be missed by virtual veterinarians.

It was veterinarians on the ground, she said, who identified the connection between dead birds at Panhandle dairies and [the Avian Influenza](https://www.texastribune.org/2025/01/31/texas-bird-flu/). Other dangers can’t be discovered through a zoom call, she said, such as the [invasive screwworm](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/texas-maneater-screwworm-warning-open-wounds/) that burrows into living animals can affect livestock, pets, and in rare cases, people.

“This presents a concrete danger to our state’s $15 billion animal agriculture industry,” Janacek said.

Johnson said he’s speaking with both rural and large animal veterinarians about the bill and is still working to evolve it so experts are more comfortable with what it does.

“Would it be nicer to have somebody in person? Yes,” Johnson said. “That’s not always practical.”

The bill was left pending in the Senate committee.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Veterinary telemedicine can lead to misdiagnosis due to limited physical examinations, with studies indicating high rates of misdiagnosis linked to telehealth consultations.
Remote assessments lack comprehensive physical evaluations, increasing risks for missed or delayed diagnoses, which could compromise animal welfare and public health.
Clients may distrust telemedicine due to perceived inadequacies in care quality, fearing that virtual consultations compromise the thoroughness of traditional veterinary visits.
